```{python}
* Only the data for year 2001 are used

* Dependent variable is 
*      ambexp   Ambulatory medical expenditures (excluding dental and outpatient mental) 
*      lambexp  Ln(ambexp) given ambexp > 0 ; missing otherwise
*      dambexp  1 if ambexp > 0 and 0 otherwise
*      lnambexp  ln(ambexp) if ambexp>0 and 0 if ambexp=0  

* Regressors are 
*  - Health insurance measures
*       ins either PPO or HMO type insurance
*  - Health status measures
*       totchr     number of chronic diseases
*  - Socioeconomic characteristics
*       age     age in years/10 
*       female   1 for females, zero otherwise
*       educ     years of schooling of decision maker
*       blhisp   either black or hispanic
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Raw data summary
use musr/mus16data.dta, clear
summarize ambexp age female educ blhisp totchr ins 
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Detailed summary to show skewness and kurtosis
summarize ambexp, detail
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Summary for positives only
summarize ambexp if ambexp >0, detail
```

### 16.3.2 Tobit analysis

```{python}
%%stata
* Tobit analysis for ambexp using all expenditures
global xlist age female educ blhisp totchr ins   //Define regressor list $xlist
tobit ambexp $xlist, ll(0) 
```

### 16.3.3 Prediction after tobit

```{python}
%%stata
* Tobit prediction and summary
predict yhatlin 
summarize yhatlin
```

### 16.3.4 Marginal effects

#### left-truncated, left-censored, and right-truncated examples

```{python}
%%stata
** Tobit postestimation

* (1) ME on censored expected value E(y|x,y>0)
quietly tobit ambexp age i.female educ i.blhisp totchr i.ins, ll(0)
margins, dydx(*) predict(e(0,.)) atmean noatlegend
```

```{python}
%%stata
* (2) ME without censoring on E(y|x)
margins, dydx(*) predict(ystar(0,.)) atmean noatlegend
```

```{python}
%%stata
* (3) ME when E(y|0<y<535)
margins, dydx(*) predict(e(0,535)) atmean noatlegend
```

#### Left-censored case computed directly

```{python}
%%stata
* Direct computation of marginal effects for E(y|x)
predict xb1, xb // xb1 is estimate of x'b
matrix btobit = e(b)
scalar sigma = btobit[1,11] // sigma is estimate of sigma
matrix bcoeff = btobit[1,1..9] // bcoeff is betas excl. constant
quietly summarize xb1 
scalar meanxb = r(mean) // mean of x'b equals (mean of x)'b 
scalar PHI = normal(meanxb/sigma) 
matrix deriv = PHI*bcoeff 
matrix list deriv 
```

```{python}
%%stata
* The following gives nicer looking results
ereturn post deriv 
ereturn display
```

#### Marginal impact on probabilities

```{python}
%%stata
* Compute margins on Pr(5000<ambexp<10000)  
quietly tobit ambexp age i.female educ i.blhisp totchr i.ins, ll(0) vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) predict(pr(5000,10000)) atmean noatlegend
```

### 16.3.5 The ivtobit command

### 16.3.6 Additional commands for censored regression

## 16.4 Tobit for lognormal data

### 16.4.1 Data example

```{python}
%%stata
* ASIDE: IN BELOW ....
* We observe  y = 0       if dy=0   and  y = y  if dy=1
* Let gamma be the smallest of the positive y's
* We define lny = gamma-0.000001 if dy=0   and lny = ln(y) if dy=1 
* This ensures that tobit, ll will correctly sort out
* the censored and uncensored observations
* Note that can't set lny = 0 if dy=1 as ln(y) < 0 if gamma
* drop xb

* Summary of log(expenditures) for positives only
summarize lambexp, detail
```

### 16.4.2 Setting the censoring point for data in logs

```{python}
%%stata
* "Tricking" Stata to handle log transformation
generate y = ambexp
generate dy = ambexp > 0
generate lny = ln(y)                // Zero values will become missing
quietly summarize lny            
scalar gamma = r(min)               // This could be negative
display "gamma = " gamma
replace lny = gamma - 0.0000001 if lny == .
tabulate y if y < 0.02              // .02 is arbitrary small value
tabulate lny if lny < gamma + 0.02 
* Label the variables
label variable y "ambexp"
label variable lny "lnambexp"
label variable dy "dambexp"
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Not included in book  
summarize y lny dy $xlist
summarize y lny dy, detail
```

### 16.4.3 Results

```{python}
%%stata
* Now do tobit on lny and calculate threshold and lambda
tobit lny $xlist, ll
```

```{python}
%%stata
* OLS, not tobit
regress lny $xlist, noheader
```

### 16.4.4 Two-limit tobit

```{python}
%%stata
* Now do two-limit tobit 
scalar upper = log(10000)
display upper
tobit lny $xlist, ll ul(9.2103404)
```

### 16.4.5 Model diagnostics

### 16.4.6 Tests of normality and homoskedasticity

#### Generalized residuals and scores

```{python}
%%stata
* Mills' ratio
quietly tobit lny $xlist, ll
predict xb, xb                            // xb is estimate of x'b
matrix btobit = e(b)
scalar sigma = btobit[1,e(df_m)+2]        // sigma is estimate of sigma
generate threshold = (gamma-xb)/sigma     // gamma: lower censoring point 
generate lambda = normalden(threshold)/normal(threshold)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Generalized residuals
* gres1 and gres2 should have mean zero by the first-order conditions
* gres3 and gres4 have mean zero if model correctly specified
* Residual (scaled by sigma) for positive values
quietly generate uifdyeq1 = (lny - xb)/sigma if dy == 1
* First-sample moment 
quietly generate double gres1 = uifdyeq1 
quietly replace gres1 = -lambda if dy == 0
summarize gres1
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Second- to fourth-sample moments
quietly generate double gres2 = uifdyeq1^2 - 1 
quietly replace gres2 = -threshold*lambda if dy == 0
quietly generate double gres3 = uifdyeq1^3 
replace gres3 = -(2 + threshold^2)*lambda if dy == 0
quietly generate double gres4 = uifdyeq1^4 - 3 
quietly replace gres4 = -(3*threshold + threshold^3)*lambda if dy == 0
```

#### Test of normality

```{python}
%%stata
* Generate the scores to use in the LM test
* These are actual scores up to a multiple constant over i
* So get same LM test as would if added the multiple
* The scores wrt b are gres1 times the relevant component of x

* Generate the scores to use in the LM test
foreach var in $xlist {
   generate score`var' = gres1*`var'
}
global scores score* gres1 gres2
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
* The score wrt sigma can be shown to be gres2
* So the scores are the score`var', gres1 (for the intercept) and gres2
* Test that calculations done correct
* All the score components should have a mean of zero
* gres3 and gres4 may not if model misspecified and these are the basis of the test
summarize $scores gres3 gres4 threshold lambda
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Test of normality in tobit regression
* NR^2 from the uncentered regression has chi-squared distribution
generate one = 1
quietly regress one gres3 gres4 $scores, noconstant
display "N R^2 = " e(N)*e(r2) " with p-value = " chi2tail(2,e(N)*e(r2))
```

#### Test of homoskedasticity

```{python}
%%stata
* Test of homoskedasticity in tobit regression
foreach var in $xlist {
   generate score2`var' = gres2*`var'
}
global scores2 score* score2* gres1 gres2
* summarize $scores2
quietly regress one gres3 gres4 $scores2, noconstant
display "N R^2 = " e(N)*e(r2) " with p-value = " chi2tail(2,e(N)*e(r2))
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
* tobcm command is deprecated - use margins to compute marginal effects after tobit
* For our example lny is left-censored at 0- but 0 is a noncensored observation
* This leads to slightly different result due to this single observation
tobit lny $xlist, ll(0)
margins, dydx(*) predict(ystar(0,.)) atmeans
```

### 16.4.7 Next step?

## 16.5 Two-part model in logs

### 16.5.1 Model structure

### 16.5.2 Part 1 specification

```{python}
%%stata
* Part 1 of the two-part model
probit dy $xlist, nolog
scalar llprobit = e(ll)
```

### 16.5.3 Part 2 of the two-part model

```{python}
%%stata
* Part 2 of the two-part model
regress lny $xlist if dy==1
scalar lllognormal = e(ll)
predict rlambexp, residuals
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Create two-part model log likelihood 
scalar lltwopart = llprobit + lllognormal  //two-part model log likelihood
display "lltwopart = " lltwopart
```

```{python}
%%stata
* hettest and sktest commands
quietly regress lny $xlist if dy==1
hettest
sktest rlambexp
```

## 16.6 Selection model

### 16.6.1 Model structure and assumptions

### 16.6.2 ML estimation of the sample-selection model

### 16.6.3 Estimation without exclusion restrictions

```{python}
%%stata
* Heckman MLE without exclusion restrictions
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist) nolog
```

### 16.6.4 Two-step estimation

```{python}
%%stata
* Heckman 2-step without exclusion restrictions
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist) twostep
```

### 16.6.5 Estimation with exclusion restrictions

```{python}
%%stata
* Heckman MLE with exclusion restriction
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist income) nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
* Heckman 2-step with exclusion restrictions
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist income) twostep
```

## 16.7 Prediction from models with outcome in logs

### 16.7.1 Predictions from tobit

```{python}
%%stata
// The following yields results that differ from book - book is in error
* Prediction from tobit on lny
generate yhat = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
generate ytrunchat = yhat / (1 - normal(threshold)) if dy==1
summarize y yhat 
summarize y yhat ytrunchat if dy==1
```

### 16.7.2 Predictions from two-part model

```{python}
%%stata
* Two-part model predictions
quietly probit dy $xlist
predict dyhat, pr
quietly regress lny $xlist if dy==1
predict xbpos, xb
generate  yhatpos = exp(xbpos+0.5*e(rmse)^2)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Unconditional prediction from two-part model
generate yhat2step = dyhat*yhatpos
summarize yhat2step y
summarize yhatpos y if dy==1
```

### 16.7.3 Predictions from selection model

```{python}
%%stata
* Heckman model predictions
quietly heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist) 
predict probpos, psel 
predict x1b1, xbsel 
predict x2b2, xb 
scalar sig2sq = e(sigma)^2 
scalar sig12sq = e(rho)*e(sigma)^2 
display "sigma1sq = 1" " sigma12sq = " sig12sq " sigma2sq = " sig2sq 
generate yhatheck = exp(x2b2 + 0.5*(sig2sq))*(1 - normal(-x1b1-sig12sq)) 
generate yhatposheck = yhatheck/probpos 
summarize yhatheck y probpos dy 
summarize yhatposheck probpos dy y if dy==1
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book 
* THE FOLLOWING SHOWS THAT THE POOR PREDICTIONS ARE DUE TO HIGH SIGMA
* THE ABOVE MODEL HAD SIGMA = 2.78
* BY CONTRAST IF WE JUST REGRESS LNY on $XLIST WITH POSITIVE OBS THEN SIGMA = 1.27 
scalar sigma = 1 
generate yhatsigma1 = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
scalar sigma = 2
generate yhatsigma2 = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
summarize yhat*
summarize yhat* if dy==1
```

## 16.8 Stata resources

## 16.9 Exercises

# 17 Count-data models

## 17.1 Introduction

## 17.2 Features of count data

### 17.2.1 Generated Poisson data

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson (mu=1) generated data
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101              // set the seed !
generate xpois= rpoisson(1) // draw from Poisson(mu=1)    
summarize xpois
tabulate xpois
```

```{python}
%%stata
histogram xpois, discrete xtitle("Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17xp, replace
```

### 17.2.2 Overdispersion and negative binomial data

```{python}
%%stata
* Negative binomial (mu=1 var=2) generated data
set seed 10101                   // set the seed !
generate xg = rgamma(1,1)
generate xnegbin = rpoisson(xg)  // NB generated as a Poisson-gamma mixture 
summarize xnegbin
tabulate xnegbin
```

```{python}
%%stata
histogram xnegbin, discrete xtitle("Negative binomial")
graph save musr/mus17negbin, replace
```

### 17.2.3 Modeling strategies

### 17.2.4 Estimation methods

## 17.3 Empirical example 1

### 17.3.1 Data summary

```{python}
%%stata
drop _all 

* Summary statistics for doctor visits data
capture use musr/mus17data.dta  
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Trying alternative path..."
    use mus17data.dta
}
global xlist private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr 
summarize docvis $xlist
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Tabulate docvis after recoding values > 10 to ranges 11-40 or 41-143
generate dvrange = docvis
recode dvrange (11/40 = 40) (41/143 = 143)
tabulate dvrange
```

### 17.3.2 Poisson model

#### Poisson model results

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson with default ML standard errors
poisson docvis $xlist, nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Squared correlation between y and yhat
drop yphat
predict yphat, n
quietly correlate docvis yphat
display "Squared correlation between y and yhat = " r(rho)^2
```

#### Robust estimate of VCE for Poisson MLE

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson with robust standard errors
poisson docvis $xlist, vce(robust) nolog  // Poisson robust SEs
```

#### Test of overdispersion

```{python}
%%stata
* Overdispersion test against V(y|x) = E(y|x) + a*{E(y|x)^2}
quietly poisson docvis $xlist, vce(robust)
predict muhat, n
quietly generate ystar = ((docvis-muhat)^2 - docvis)/muhat
regress ystar muhat, noconstant noheader
```

#### Coefficient interpretation and marginal effects

```{python}
%%stata
* Average marginal effect for Poisson
quietly poisson docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*)
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
* Poisson as glm
glm docvis $xlist, family(poisson) link(log) robust  // glm
```

### 17.3.3 NB2 model

#### NB2 model results

```{python}
%%stata
* Standard negative binomial (NB2) with default SEs
nbreg docvis $xlist, nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Squared correlation between y and yhat
predict ynbhat, n
quietly correlate docvis ynbhat
display "Squared correlation between y and yhat = " r(rho)^2
```

#### Fitted probabilities for Poisson and NB2 models

#### The countfit command

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson: Sample vs avg predicted probabilities of y = 0, 1, ..., 5 
* countfit is deprecated - use manual calculation of predicted probabilities
quietly poisson docvis $xlist
predict phat, n
forvalues i = 0/5 {
    quietly count if docvis == `i'
    local actual`i' = r(N) / _N
    quietly generate double prob`i' = exp(-phat) * phat^`i' / exp(lnfactorial(`i'))
    quietly summarize prob`i'
    local pred`i' = r(mean)
    display "Count `i': Actual = " %6.4f `actual`i'' "  Predicted = " %6.4f `pred`i''
}
drop phat prob*
```

```{python}
%%stata
* NB2: Sample vs average predicted probabilities of y = 0, 1, ..., 5 
* countfit is deprecated - use manual calculation for negative binomial
quietly nbreg docvis $xlist
predict phat, n
local alpha = e(alpha)
forvalues i = 0/5 {
    quietly count if docvis == `i'
    local actual`i' = r(N) / _N
    quietly generate double prob`i' = exp(lngamma(`i' + 1/`alpha') - lngamma(`i' + 1) - lngamma(1/`alpha')) * (1/(1 + `alpha'*phat))^(1/`alpha') * (`alpha'*phat/(1 + `alpha'*phat))^`i'
    quietly summarize prob`i'
    local pred`i' = r(mean)
    display "Count `i': Actual = " %6.4f `actual`i'' "  Predicted = " %6.4f `pred`i''
}
drop phat prob*
```

#### The prvalue command

```{python}
%%stata
* NB2: Predicted NB2 probabilities at x = x* of y = 0, 1, ..., 5 
* prvalue is deprecated - manually calculate predicted probabilities
quietly nbreg docvis $xlist
local alpha = e(alpha)
predict xb, xb
quietly replace xb = exp(xb) if !missing(xb)
forvalues i = 0/5 {
    quietly generate double prob`i' = exp(lngamma(`i' + 1/`alpha') - lngamma(`i' + 1) - lngamma(1/`alpha')) * (1/(1 + `alpha'*xb))^(1/`alpha') * (`alpha'*xb/(1 + `alpha'*xb))^`i' if private==1 & medicaid==1
    quietly summarize prob`i' if private==1 & medicaid==1
    display "Pr(y=`i' | private=1, medicaid=1) = " %6.4f r(mean)
    drop prob`i'
}
drop xb
```

#### Discussion

#### Generalized NB model

```{python}
%%stata
* Generalized negative binomial with alpha parameterized
capture noisily gnbreg docvis $xlist, lnalpha(female bh) nolog
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: gnbreg convergence failed"
}
```

### 17.3.4 Nonlinear least-squares estimation

```{python}
%%stata
* Nonlinear least squares
capture noisily nl (docvis = exp({xb: $xlist one})), vce(robust) nolog
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: nl convergence failed"
}
```

### 17.3.5 Hurdle model

```{python}
%%stata
* Hurdle: Pr(y=0)=pi and Pr(y=k)=(1-pi) x Poisson(2) truncated at 0
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101             // set the seed !
scalar pi=1-(1-exp(-2))/2  // Probability y=0
generate xhurdle = 0
scalar minx = 0
while minx == 0 {
 generate xph = rpoisson(2) 
 quietly replace xhurdle = xph if xhurdle==0 
 drop xph
 quietly summarize xhurdle
 scalar minx = r(min)
}
replace xhurdle = 0 if runiform() < pi
summarize xhurdle
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
histogram xhurdle, discrete xtitle("Hurdle Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17hurdle, replace 
```

#### Variants of the hurdle model

#### Application of the hurdle model

```{python}
%%stata
* Hurdle logit-nb model manually
logit docvis $xlist, nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Second step uses positives only
summarize docvis if docvis > 0
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Zero-truncated negative binomial 
ztnb docvis $xlist if docvis>0, nolog 
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
* hnblogit command is not available - hurdle model already estimated above manually
* Using logit for hurdle and nbreg for positive counts
display "Note: hnblogit command not available, use manual hurdle estimation shown above"
```

```{python}
%%stata
* margins for marginal effects of first part
quietly logit docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean noatlegend
```

```{python}
%%stata
* margins for marginal effects of second part
quietly ztnb docvis i.private i.medicaid age age2 educyr i.actlim totchr if docvis>0, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean noatlegend
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following repeats earlier code as xpois and xnegbin have since been dropped
* Poisson (mu=1) and NB(1,1) generated data
quietly set obs 10000
set seed 10101                   // set the seed !
generate xpois= rpoisson(1) 
generate xg = rgamma(1,1)
generate xnegbin = rpoisson(xg) // NB draws
```

#### Simulated FMM sample with comparisons

```{python}
%%stata
* Mixture: Poisson(.5) with prob .9 and Poisson(5.5) with prob .1
set seed 10101        // set the seed !
generate xp1= rpoisson(.5)
generate xp2= rpoisson(5.5)
summarize xp1 xp2
rename xp1 xpmix
quietly replace xpmix = xp2 if runiform() > 0.9
summarize xpmix
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Tabulate the mixture data
tabulate xpmix
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not included in book
histogram xpmix, discrete xtitle("Finite mixture Poisson")
graph save musr/mus17pmix, replace
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Compare the four distributions, all with mean 1
graph combine musr/mus17xp.gph musr/mus17negbin.gph musr/mus17pmix.gph ///
musr/mus17hurdle.gph, title("Four different distributions with mean = 1") ///
ycommon xcommon
```

```{python}
%%stata
graph export musr/mus17countdist.eps, replace
```

#### ML estimation of the FMM

#### The fmm command

#### Application: Poisson finite-mixture model

```{python}
%%stata
* Compare the four distributions, all with mean 1
summarize xpois xnegbin xpmix xhurdle
```

#### Interpretation

```{python}
%%stata
* Finite-mixture model using fmm command with constant probabilities
use musr/mus17data.dta, clear
fmm 2, vce(robust): poisson docvis $xlist
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Predict y for two components
quietly fmm 2, vce(robust): poisson docvis $xlist
estat lcmean
predict yfit*, mu
summarize yfit*
```

#### Comparing marginal effects

```{python}
%%stata
* Marginal effects for component 1
margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(1)) atmean noatlegend
* Marginal effects for component 2 
margins, dydx(*) predict(mu class(2)) atmean noatlegend
```

```{python}
%%stata
set scheme s1mono  /* Graphics scheme */
* Create histograms of fitted values  
quietly histogram yfit1, name(_comp_1, replace)
quietly histogram yfit2, name(_comp_2, replace)
quietly graph combine _comp_1 _comp_2
```

```{python}
%%stata
quietly graph export musr/fitted_comp.eps, replace
```

#### Application: NB finite-mixture model

```{python}
%%stata
* 2-component mixture of NB1 
fmm 2, vce(robust): nbreg docvis $xlist, dispersion(mean)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Fitted values for 2-component NB1 mixture
capture drop yfit1 yfit2 
predict yfit*, mu
summarize yfit*
```

#### Model selection

#### Cautionary note

## 17.4 Empirical example 2

### 17.4.1 Zero-inflated data

```{python}
%%stata
drop _all

*set more off
use musr/mus17data_z.dta 
* Summary stats for ER use model
use musr/mus17data_z.dta      
global xlist1 age actlim totchr 
summarize er $xlist1
tabulate er 
```

### 17.4.2 Models for zero-inflated data

### 17.4.3 Results for the N B2 model

```{python}
%%stata
* NB2 for er
nbreg er $xlist1, nolog
```

#### The prcounts command

```{python}
%%stata
* Sample average fitted probabilities of y = 0 to max() 
* prcounts is deprecated - calculate manually using predict
predict xb, xb
local alpha = e(alpha)
forvalues i = 0/3 {
    generate erpr`i' = exp(lngamma(`i' + 1/`alpha') - lngamma(`i' + 1) - lngamma(1/`alpha')) * (1/(1 + `alpha'*exp(xb)))^(1/`alpha') * (`alpha'*exp(xb)/(1 + `alpha'*exp(xb)))^`i'
}
drop xb
summarize erpr*
```

### 17.4.4 Results for ZINB

```{python}
%%stata
* Zero-inflated negative binomial for er
zinb er $xlist1, inflate($xlist1) forcevuong nolog
```

### 17.4.5 Model comparison

#### The countfit command

#### Model comparison using countfit

```{python}
%%stata
* Comparison of NB and ZINB using manual probability calculations
* countfit is deprecated - estimate both models and compare fit manually
quietly nbreg er $xlist1
estimates store NB
quietly zinb er $xlist1, inflate($xlist1)
estimates store ZINB
* Compare AIC and BIC
estimates stats NB ZINB
```

## 17.5 Models with endogenous regressors

### 17.5.1 Structural-model approach

#### Model and assumptions

#### Two-step estimation

#### Application

```{python}
%%stata
clear all
* First-stage linear regression
use musr/mus17data.dta
global xlist2 medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr
regress private $xlist2 income ssiratio, vce(robust)
predict lpuhat, residual
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Second-stage Poisson with robust SEs
poisson docvis private $xlist2 lpuhat, vce(robust) nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Program and bootstrap for Poisson two-step estimator
program endogtwostep, eclass
  version 11
  tempname b
  capture drop lpuhat2
  regress private $xlist2 income ssiratio
  predict lpuhat2, residual
  poisson docvis private $xlist2 lpuhat2
  matrix `b' = e(b)
  ereturn post `b'
end
bootstrap _b, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots nowarn: endogtwostep
```

### 17.5.2 Nonlinear IV method

```{python}
%%stata
* Nonlinear IV estimator for Poisson: computation using optimize
generate cons = 1
local y docvis
local xlist private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr cons
local zlist income ssiratio medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr cons
* Mata code to obtain GMM estimator for Poisson using command optimize
mata 
  void pgmm(todo, b, y, X, Z, Qb, g, H)
  {
    Xb = X*b'
    mu = exp(Xb)
    h = Z'(y-mu)
    W = cholinv(cross(Z,Z))
    Qb = h'W*h
    if (todo == 0) return
    G = -(mu:*Z)'X
    g = (G'W*h)'
    if (todo == 1) return
    H = G'W*G
    _makesymmetric(H)
   }
  st_view(y=., ., "`y'")  
  st_view(X=., ., tokens("`xlist'"))
  st_view(Z=., ., tokens("`zlist'"))
  S = optimize_init()
  optimize_init_which(S,"min")
  optimize_init_evaluator(S, &pgmm())
  optimize_init_evaluatortype(S, "d2")
  optimize_init_argument(S, 1, y)
  optimize_init_argument(S, 2, X)
  optimize_init_argument(S, 3, Z)
  optimize_init_params(S, J(1,cols(X),0))
  optimize_init_technique(S,"nr")
  b = optimize(S)  
  // Compute robust estimate of VCE and SEs
  Xb = X*b'
  mu = exp(Xb)
  h = Z'(y-mu)
  W = cholinv(cross(Z,Z))
  G = -(mu:*Z)'X
  Shat = ((y-mu):*Z)'((y-mu):*Z)*rows(X)/(rows(X)-cols(X))
  Vb = luinv(G'W*G)*G'W*Shat*W*G*luinv(G'W*G)
  *seb = (sqrt(diagonal(Vb)))'
  *b \ seb
  st_matrix("b",b)
  st_matrix("Vb",Vb) 
end
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Nonlinear IV estimator for Poisson: formatted results
matrix colnames b = `xlist'
matrix colnames Vb = `xlist'
matrix rownames Vb = `xlist'
ereturn post b Vb
ereturn display
```

## 17.6 Stata resources

## 17.7 Exercises

```{python}
%%stata
* Command gmm for one-step GMM (nonlinear IV) of overidentified Poisson model
gmm (docvis - exp({xb:private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr}+{b0})), ///
 instruments(income ssiratio medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr) onestep nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Command gmm for two-step GMM (nonlinear IV) of overidentified Poisson model
gmm (docvis - exp({xb:private medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr}+{b0})), ///
 instruments(income ssiratio medicaid age age2 educyr actlim totchr) twostep nolog
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Test of overidentifying restriction following two-step GMM
estat overid
```

# 18 Nonlinear panel models

## 18.1 Introduction

## 18.2 Nonlinear panel-data overview

### 18.2.1 Some basic nonlinear panel models

#### FE models

#### RE models

#### Pooled models or population-averaged models

#### Comparison of models

### 18.2.2 Dynamic Models

### 18.2.3 Stata nonlinear panel commands

## 18.3 Nonlinear panel-data example

### 18.3.1 Data description and summary statistics

```{python}
%%stata
********** DATA DESCRIPTION **********

* mus18data.dta    
* Rand Health Insurance Experiment data 
* Essentially same data as in P. Deb and P.K. Trivedi (2002)
* "The Structure of Demand for Medical Care: Latent Class versus
* Two-Part Models", Journal of Health Economics, 21, 601-625
* except that paper used different outcome (counts rather than $)
* Each observation is for an individual over a year.
* Individuals may appear in up to five years.
* All available sample is used except only fee for service plans included.
* If panel data used then clustering is on id (person id)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Describe dependent variables and regressors
use musr/mus18data.dta, clear
describe dmdu med mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child id year
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Summarize dependent variables and regressors
summarize dmdu med mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child id year
```

### 18.3.2 Panel-data organization

```{python}
%%stata
* Panel description of dataset 
xtset id year
xtdescribe
```

### 18.3.3 Within and between variation

```{python}
%%stata
* Panel summary of time-varying regressors
xtsum age lfam child
```

### 18.3.4 FE or RE model for these data?

## 18.4 Binary outcome models

### 18.4.1 Panel summary of the dependent variable

```{python}
%%stata
* Panel summary of dependent variable
xtsum dmdu
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Year-to-year transitions in whether visit doctor
xttrans dmdu
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Correlations in the dependent variable
corr dmdu l.dmdu l2.dmdu
```

### 18.4.2 Pooled logit estimator

```{python}
%%stata
* Logit cross-section with panel-robust standard errors
logit dmdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, vce(cluster id) nolog
```

### 18.4.3 The xtlogit command

### 18.4.4 The xtgee command

### 18.4.5 PA logit estimator

```{python}
%%stata
* Pooled logit cross-section with exchangeable errors and panel-robust VCE
xtlogit dmdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, pa corr(exch) vce(robust) nolog
```

### 18.4.6 RE logit estimator

```{python}
%%stata
* Logit random-effects estimator
xtlogit dmdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re nolog
```

### 18.4.7 FE logit estimator

```{python}
%%stata
* Logit fixed-effects estimator
xtlogit dmdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, fe nolog 
```

### 18.4.8 Panel logit estimator comparison

```{python}
%%stata
* Logit mixed-effects estimator (same as xtlogit, re)
*xtmelogit dmdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child || id:
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Panel logit estimator comparison
global xlist lcoins ndisease female age lfam child
quietly logit dmdu $xlist, vce(cluster id)
estimates store POOLED
quietly xtlogit dmdu $xlist, pa corr(exch) vce(robust)
estimates store PA
quietly xtlogit dmdu $xlist, re     // SEs are not cluster-robust
estimates store RE
quietly xtlogit dmdu $xlist, fe     // SEs are not cluster-robust
estimates store FE
estimates table POOLED PA RE FE, equations(1) se b(%8.4f) stats(N ll) stfmt(%8.0f)
```

### 18.4.9 Prediction and marginal effects

### 18.4.10 Mixed-effects logit estimator

```{python}
%%stata
*xtmelogit dmdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child || id:
```

## 18.5 Tobit model

### 18.5.1 Panel summary of the dependent variable

```{python}
%%stata
* Panel summary of dependent variable
xtsum med
```

### 18.5.2 RE tobit model

```{python}
%%stata
* Tobit random-effects estimator
xttobit med lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, ll(0) nolog
```

### 18.5.3 Generalized tobit models

### 18.5.4 Parametric nonlinear panel models

## 18.6 Count-data models

### 18.6.1 The xtpoisson command

### 18.6.2 Panel summary of the dependent variable

```{python}
%%stata
* Panel summary of dependent variable
xtsum mdu
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Year-to-year transitions in doctor visits
generate mdushort = mdu
replace mdushort = 4 if mdu >= 4
xttrans mdushort
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Not included in book
corr mdu L.mdu
```

### 18.6.3 Pooled Poisson estimator

```{python}
%%stata
* Pooled Poisson estimator with cluster-robust standard errors
poisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, vce(cluster id)
```

### 18.6.4 PA Poisson esil:imator

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson PA estimator with unstructured error correlation and robust VCE
xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, pa corr(unstr) vce(robust)
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Not included in book
* Poisson random-effects estimator with default standard errors
xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re
```

### 18.6.5 RE Poisson estimators

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson random-effects estimator with cluster-robust standard errors
*xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re vce(boot, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots)
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Not included in book
* Poisson random-effects estimator with normal intercept and default standard errors
xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re normal
estimates store poisrenormal
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Not included in book
* Poisson random-effects estimator with normal intercept and normal slope for one parameter
*mepoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child || id: ndisease
*estimates store poisrenormrob
```

### 18.6.6 FE Poisson estimator

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson fixed-effects estimator with default standard errors
*xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, fe i(id)
*mepoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, fe i(id) 
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Poisson fixed-effects estimator with cluster-robust standard errors
*xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, fe vce(boot, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots)
```

### 18.6.7 Panel Poisson estimators comparison

```{python}
%%stata
/*
* Comparison of Poisson panel estimators
quietly xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, pa corr(unstr) vce(robust)
estimates store PPA_ROB
quietly xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re
estimates store PRE
quietly xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re normal
estimates store PRE_NORM
quietly xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, fe
estimates store PFE
estimates table PPA_ROB PRE PRE_NORM PFE, equations(1) b(%8.4f) se stats(N ll) stfmt(%8.0f)
*/
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Not included in book
* Negative binomial pooled estimator with default standard errors
nbreg mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Negative binomial pooled estimator with het robust standard errors
nbreg mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, vce(robust)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Negative binomial pooled estimator with cluster-robust standard errors
nbreg mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, cluster(id)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Negative binomial population-averaged estimator with equicorrelated errors
xtnbreg lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, pa corr(exch) vce(robust)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Negative binomial random-effects estimator with default standard errors
xtnbreg mdu ndisease female age lfam child, re i(id) 
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Negative binomial fixed-effects estimator with default standard errors
xtnbreg mdu ndisease female age lfam child, fe i(id) 
```

### 18.6.8 Negative binomial estimators

```{python}
%%stata
* Comparison of negative binomial panel estimators
quietly xtpoisson mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, pa corr(exch) vce(robust)
estimates store PPA_ROB
quietly xtnbreg mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, pa corr(exch) vce(robust)
estimates store NBPA_ROB
quietly xtnbreg mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, re
estimates store NBRE
quietly xtnbreg mdu lcoins ndisease female age lfam child, fe
estimates store NBFE
estimates table PPA_ROB NBPA_ROB NBRE NBFE, equations(1) b(%8.4f) se stats(N ll) stfmt(%8.0f)
```

## 18.7 Stata resources

## 18.8 Exercises

# A Programming in Stata

## A.1 Stata matrix commands

### A.1.1 Stata matrix overview

### A.1.2 Stata matrix input and output

#### Matrix input by hand

```{python}
%%stata
* Define a matrix explicitly and list the matrix
matrix define A = (1,2,3 \ 4,5,6)
matrix list A
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Matrix row and column names
matrix rownames A = one two
matrix list A
```

#### Matrix input from Stata estimation results

```{python}
%%stata
clear all
* Read in data, summarize and run regression
use musr/mus03data.dta
keep if _n <= 100
drop if ltotexp == . | totchr == .
summarize ltotexp totchr  
regress ltotexp totchr, noheader 
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Create a matrix from estimation results
matrix vbols = e(V)
matrix list vbols
```

### A.1.3 Stata matrix subscripts and combining matrices

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not in book
* Create a matrix from variables
mkmat ltotexp, matrix(y)
generate intercept = 1
mkmat totchr intercept, matrix(X)
```

```{python}
%%stata
// Following not in book
* Convert a matrix into variables
svmat X
summarize X*
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Change value of an entry in matrix
matrix X[1,1] = X[1,2]
matrix list X
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Select part of matrix (all rows, column 2)
matrix B = X[1...,2]
matrix list B
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Add columns to an existing matrix
matrix C = B, B
matrix list C
```

### A.1.4 Matrix operators

```{python}
%%stata
* Matrix operators
matrix D = C + 3*C
matrix list D
```

### A.1.5 Matrix functions

```{python}
%%stata
* Matrix functions
matrix r = rowsof(D)
matrix list r
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Can use scalar if 1x1 matrix
scalar ralt = rowsof(D)
display ralt
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Inverse of nonsymmetric square matrix
* Create a square matrix from X'X
matrix XtX = X'*X
matrix XtXinv = inv(XtX)
matrix list XtXinv
```

### A.1.6 Matrix accumulation commands

```{python}
%%stata
* OLS estimator using X and y and matrix operators
matrix bols = (invsym(X'*X))*(X'*y)
matrix list bols
```

### A.1.7 OLS using Stata matrix commands

```{python}
%%stata
* OLS estimator using matrix accumulation operators
matrix accum XTX = totchr              // Form X'X including constant
matrix vecaccum yTX = ltotexp totchr   // Form y'X including constant
matrix cols = invsym(XTX)*(yTX)'
matrix list cols
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Illustrate Stata matrix commands: OLS with output
matrix accum XTX = totchr            // Form X'X including constant
matrix vecaccum yTX = ltotexp totchr // Form y'X including constant
matrix b = invsym(XTX)*(yTX)'
matrix accum yTy = ltotexp, noconstant
scalar k = rowsof(XTX)
scalar n = _N
matrix s2 = (yTy - b'*XTX'*b)/(n-k)
matrix V = s2*invsym(XTX)
matrix list b
matrix list V
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Stata matrix commands to compute SEs and t statistics given b and V
matrix se = (vecdiag(cholesky(diag(vecdiag(V)))))'
matrix seinv = (vecdiag(cholesky(invsym(diag(vecdiag(V))))))'
matrix t = hadamard(b,seinv)
matrix results = b, se, t
matrix colnames results = coeff sterror tratio
matrix list results, format(%7.0g)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Easier is to use ereturn post and display given b and V
matrix brow = b'
ereturn post brow V
ereturn display
```

## A.2 Programs

### A.2.1 Simple programs (no arguments or access to results)

```{python}
%%stata
* Program with no arguments
program time
  display c(current_time) c(current_date)
end
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Run the program
time
```

### A.2.2 Modifying a program

```{python}
%%stata
* Drop program if it already exists, write program and run
capture program drop time
program time
  display c(current_time) c(current_date)
end
time
```

### A.2.3 Programs with positional arguments

```{python}
%%stata
* Program with two positional arguments
program meddiff
  tempvar diff
  generate `diff' = `1' - `2'
  _pctile `diff', p(50)
  display "Median difference = " r(r1)
end
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Run the program with two arguments
meddiff ltotexp totchr
```

### A.2.4 Temporary variables

### A.2.5 Programs with named positional a􀂗guments

```{python}
%%stata
* Program with two named positional arguments
capture program drop meddiff
program meddiff
  args y x
  tempvar diff
  generate `diff' = `y' - `x'
  _pctile `diff', p(50)
  display "Median difference = " r(r1)
end
meddiff ltotexp totchr
```

### A.2.6 Storing and retrieving program results

```{python}
%%stata
* Program with results stored in r()
capture program drop meddiff
program meddiff, rclass
  args y x
  tempvar diff
  generate `diff' = `y' - `x'
  _pctile `diff', p(50)
  return scalar medylx = r(r1)
end
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Running the program does not immediately display the result
meddiff ltotexp totchr
return list
display r(medylx)
```

### A.2.7 Programs with arguments using standard Stata syntax

```{python}
%%stata
regress ltotexp totchr if ltotexp < . in 1/100, vce (robust)
```

```{python}
%%stata
*myols ltotexp totchr if ltotexp < . in 1/100, vce (robust)
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Program that uses Stata commands syntax and gettoken to parse arguments
program myols
  syntax varlist [if] [in] [,vce(string)]
  gettoken y xvars : varlist
  display "varlist contains: "  "`varlist'" 
  display "and  if contains: "  "`if'" 
  display "and  in contains: "  "`in'"
  display "and vce contains: "  "`vce'"
  display "and   y contains: "  "`y'"
  display "& xvars contains: "  "`xvars'"
  regress `y' `xvars' `if' `in', `vce' noheader
end
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Execute program myols for an example 
myols ltotexp totchr if ltotexp < . in 1/100, vce(robust)
```

### A.2.8 Ado-files

```{python}
%%stata
* Example of an ado-file
capture program drop meddiff
*! version 1.1.0  05oct2009
program meddiff, rclass
  version 11
  args y x 
  tempvar diff
  quietly {
          generate double `diff' = `y' - `x'
          _pctile `diff', p(50)
           return scalar medylx = r(r1)
  }
  display "Median of first variable - second variable = " r(r1)
end
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Execute program meddiff for an example  
meddiff ltotexp totchr
```

## A.3 Program debugging

### A.3.1 Some simple tips

```{python}
%%stata
* Display intermediate output to aid debugging
matrix accum XTX = totchr            // Recall constant is added
matrix list XTX                      // Should be 2 x 2 
matrix vecaccum yTX = ltotexp totchr
matrix list yTX                      // Should be 1 x 2
matrix bOLS = invsym(XTX)*(yTX)'
matrix list bOLS                     // Should be 2 x 1
```

### A.3.2 Error messages and return code

```{python}
%%stata
* Debug an initial nonprogram version of a program
tempvar y x diff 
generate `y' = ltotexp
generate `x' = totchr
generate double `diff' = `y' - `x'
_pctile `diff', p(50)
scalar medylx = r(r1)
display "Median of first variable - second variable = " medylx
```

### A.3.3 Trace

```{python}
%%stata
set trace on
set trace off
```

# B Mata

## B.1 How to run Mata

### B.1.1 Mata commands in Mata

```{python}
%%stata
* Read in data
clear all
use musr/mus03data.dta
keep if _n <= 100
generate cons = 1
```

```{python}
%%stata
* Sample Mata session
mata
I = I(2)  
I
end
```

### B.1.2 Mata commands in Stata

```{python}
%%stata
* Mata commands issued from Stata
mata: I = I(2)
mata: I
```

### B.1.3 Stata commands in Mata

```{python}
%%stata
* Stata commands issued from Mata
mata
stata("summarize ltotexp")
end
```

### B.1.4 lnteractive versus batch use

### B.1.5 Mata help

```{python}
%%stata
* Mata help with output not included in book
mata
help mata det
help m4 matrix
help mata
end
```

## B.2 Mata matrix commands

### B.2.1 Mata matrix input

#### Matrix input by hand

```{python}
%%stata
mata  
// Create a matrix
A = (1,2,3 \ 4,5,6)
// List a matrix
A
end
```

#### Identity matrices, unit vectors, and matrices of constants

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Create a 2x2 identity matrix
I = I(2)

// Create a 1x5 unit row vector with 1 in second entry and zeros elsewhere
e = e(2,5)
e

// Create a 2x5 matrix with entry 3 
J = J(2,5,3)
J

// Create a row vector with entries 8 to 15
a = 8..15
a
end
```

#### Matrix input from Stata data

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Associate a Mata matrix with variables stored in Stata
st_view(y=., ., "ltotexp")
st_view(X=., ., ("totchr", "cons"))

// Create a Mata matrix from variables stored in Stata
Xloaded = st_data(., ("totchr", "cons"))
Xloaded
end
```

#### Matrix input from Stata matrix

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Read Stata matrix (created in first line below) into Mata
stata("matrix define B = I(2)")
C = st_matrix("B")
C

// Transfer Mata matrix to Stata
st_matrix("D",C)
stata("matrix list D")
end
```

#### Stata interface functions

### B.2.2 Mata matrix operators

#### Element-by-element operators

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Element by element multiplication of matrix by column vector
b = 2::3
J = J(2,5,3)
b:*J
end
```

### B.2.3 Mata functions

#### Scalar and matrix functions

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Matrix function that returns scalar
r = rows(A)
r

// Matrix function that returns matrix by element-by-element transformation
D = sqrt(A)
D

// Calculate eigenvalues and eigenvectors
E = (1, 2 \ 4, 3)
lambda = .
eigvecs = .
eigensystem(E,eigvecs,lambda)
lambda
eigvecs
end
```

#### Matrix inversion

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Matrix inversion: Use of makesymmetric() before cholinv()
F = 0.5*I(2)
G = makesymmetric(cholinv(F'F))
G
end
```

### B.2.4 Mata cross products

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Matrix cross product
beta = (cholinv(cross(X,X)))*(cross(X,y))
beta
end
```

### B.2.5 Mata matrix subscripts and combining matrices

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Matrix subscripts
A[1,2] = A[1,1]
A

// Combining matrices: add columns
M = A, A
M

// Combining matrices: add rows
N = A \ A
N

// Form submatrix using list subscripts
O = M[(1\2), (5::6)]
O

// Form submatrix using range subscripts
P = M[|1,5 \ 2,6|]
P
end
```

### B.2.6 Transferring Mata data and matrices to Stata

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Output mata matrix to Stata
st_matrix("Q", P)
stata("matrix list Q")
end
```

#### Creating Stata data from a Mata vector

```{python}
%%stata
mata
// Output mata matrix to Stata
yhat = X*beta
st_addvar("float", "ltotexphat")
st_store(.,"ltotexphat", yhat)
stata("summarize ltotexp ltotexphat")

end 
```

## B.3 Programming in Mata

### B.3.1 Declarations

```{python}
%%stata
mata
  void poissonmle(real scalar todo, 
    real rowvector b,
    real colvector y, 
    real matrix X,
    real colvector lndensity,
    real matrix g,
    real matrix H)
  {
    real colvector Xb
    real colvector mu
    Xb = X*b'
    mu = exp(Xb)
    lndensity = -mu + y:*Xb - lnfactorial(y)
    if (todo == 0) return
    g = (y-mu):*X   
    if (todo == 1) return
    H = - cross(X, mu, X)
  }
end
```

### B.3.2 Mata program

```{python}
%%stata
mata:
  void calcsum(varname, resultissum)  
  {
    st_view(x=., ., varname)
    resultissum = colsum(x)
  } 
  sum = .
  calcsum("ltotexp", sum)
  sum
end 
```

### B.3.3 Mata program with results output to Stata

```{python}
%%stata
mata:
  void function calcsum2(varname)  
  {
    st_view(x=., ., varname)
    st_numscalar("r(sum)",colsum(x))
  } 
  calcsum2("ltotexp")
  stata("display r(sum)")
end 
```

### B.3.4 Stata program that calls a Mata program

```{python}
%%stata
program varsum
  version 11
  syntax varname
  mata: calcsum2("`varlist'")
  display r(sum)
end
varsum ltotexp
```

### B.3.5 Using Mata in ado-files
